mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and backup, making it ideal for users who want to manage MySQL databases efficiently in a cloud-native way. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with Kubernetes and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the MySQL Operator Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install the operator using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command registers the MySQL Operator repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the MySQL Operator in a new namespace called 'mysql-operator'.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace

```

> Installs the MySQL Operator with default settings.

steps.title: Verify the Operator Installation
steps.description:
Check if the MySQL Operator is running correctly by querying the deployment status.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> You should see the operator deployment with a READY status.

steps.title: Create a Secret for MySQL Credentials
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the root user credentials for MySQL, which the operator will use to manage the database.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword='sakila'

```

> Replace 'sakila' with your desired root password.

steps.title: Define a MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This YAML configuration defines a MySQL InnoDB Cluster that the operator will manage.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

```

> This creates a cluster named 'mycluster' using the previously created secret.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

steps.title: Verify the InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
Check the status of the InnoDB Cluster to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com mycluster

```

> You should see the cluster status and details.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete the MySQL InnoDB Cluster and any data stored within it.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command removes the MySQL Operator deployment.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your MySQL data. This step will upgrade the MySQL Operator to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com -o yaml > mycluster-backup.yaml

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backup the current InnoDB Cluster configuration.
> This command upgrades the MySQL Operator to the latest version.
> Verify that the operator is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL Operator may not have sufficient permissions to create resources.

**Fix:**
Ensure the operator has the necessary RBAC permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Secret not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified secret for MySQL credentials does not exist.

**Fix:**
Create the secret using the correct command.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'MySQL server is not responding'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL pods may not be running or are in a crash loop.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have completed its initialization.

**Fix:**
Wait a few moments and check the status again with 'kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com'.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.4 features and enhancements.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new MySQL 9.0 features including improved performance and security enhancements.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Ensure your MySQL instances are upgraded to at least version 8.4 before migrating.

resolution.summary: You should see the InnoDB Cluster status as 'Ready' when everything is working correctly, e.g., 'kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com mycluster'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources.
